segments.s140-g57: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause here ('when' / 'if'). The clause 'رستم پدر باشد' uses a nominal predicate with the copula باشد implied as 'is'; the coordinate 'و من پسر' omits a repeated copula (literally 'and I (am) son'), a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s140-g58: نباید is an impersonal negative modal ('there should not / must not be'). 'به گیتی' is a locative phrase 'in the world', placed before the indefinite subject 'کسی تاجور' (literally 'anyone trader'), with no overt copula — the whole means 'there ought not to be anyone in the world who is a trader.'
segments.s140-g59: چو = 'when'. The adjective روشن ('bright') is predicated with the past copula بود ('was'), and the phrase 'روی خورشید و ماه' ('the face of the sun and the moon') is the subject; the verb follows the predicate in this poetic word order.
segments.s140-g60: ستاره is the subject 'a star.' چرا is a rhetorical interrogative 'why.' برفرازد is a present/subjunctive-type form meaning 'would raise / should raise / lifts up,' and کلاه is the object 'hat' (here figuratively 'a cap/crown'); together they ask why a lesser luminary should raise a hat when sun and moon already shine.
segments.s140-g61: ز هر سو = 'from every side/direction.' سپه شد literally 'became a host' (شد 'became') and انجمن ('assembly') functions as the result/identification; براو (بِ + او) is the directional clitic 'toward him / around him.' The clause describes forces assembling around him.
segments.s140-g62: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'انجمن.' The correlative هم ... هم ... links two predicates: باگهر 'adorned/jeweled' and تیغ‌زن 'sword-wielding/swordsmen.' بود is the past copula 'was' connecting both adjectives to the assembly.
segments.s140-g41: چنین گفت سهراب is a fixed word order meaning 'Sohrab said thus/so' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said'); کاندر جهان is an archaic compound (کاندر = که اندر) meaning 'in the world' and functions as an adverbial phrase specifying scope. The verb phrase 'چنین گفت' introduces the reported content that follows.
segments.s140-g42: کسی این سخن را ندارد نهان: کسی is an indefinite pronoun here with negative force ('no one'); این سخن را is the definite direct object marked by را (fronted for emphasis), and ندارد is the present-tense verb 'does not have/keep'. نهان postposes as a predicate/adverb meaning 'hidden' (i.e. 'keeps ... hidden').
segments.s140-g43: بزرگان جنگ‌آور is a nominal phrase 'the great/worthy ones who are warriors' (بزرگان = 'great ones', جنگ‌آور = 'warrior'), and از باستان ('from antiquity') is a prepositional/temporal phrase qualifying them. The order places the descriptive elements before the verb in typical epic diction.
segments.s140-g44: ز رستم زنند این زمان داستان: ز (poetic from/about = از) + رستم marks the theme 'about Rostam'; زنند is the classical 3rd-person-plural present of زدن (here used idiomatically with داستان to mean 'they tell (a tale)') — it is visually split into زن + ند in the tokens but functions as one verbal form; این زمان is a temporal adverbial 'now/at this time'; داستان is the object 'tale/story'.
segments.s140-g45: نبرده نژادی که چونین بود: نبرده is an archaic negative perfect/compound meaning 'has not borne/produced' (here 'has not given rise to'); نژادی refers to 'a race/lineage' (used substantively), و که چونین بود is a relative clause 'that was such'. Word order here places the negative verb before the noun in elevated diction.
segments.s140-g46: نهان کردن از من چه آیین بود: نهان کردن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to hide' (نهان + کردن with کردن as the light verb), از من 'from me' indicates the beneficiary/victim of the hiding, and چه آیین بود is a rhetorical question 'what custom/usage was (this)?' with بود as past copula.
segments.s140-g47: کنون من ز ترکان جنگ‌آوران places the temporal adverb کنون 'now' up front, then the subject من 'I'; ز (poetic از) 'from' marks origin, followed by ترکان 'the Turks' and جنگ‌آوران 'warriors' as the group to be drawn from them. The phrase sets up the source and agents for the following verb.
segments.s140-g48: فراز آورم لشکری بی‌کران: فراز is an adverb/adverbial 'on high/aloft' or 'up', آورم is 1st-singular present (classical) 'I bring' with future force here, and لشکری بی‌کران is the object 'an army/host boundless' (لشکری = troops/army, بی‌کران = endless). The verb precedes its object in this elevated construction.
segments.s140-g1: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نه ماه بگذشت is a time-clause literally 'nine months passed' (بگذشت = past of 'to pass' used for time). بر دخت شاه places that lapse 'upon/for the king's daughter' (بر + NP marks the person affected). The whole segment means 'when nine months had passed for the king's daughter'.
segments.s140-g2: یکی introduces an indefinite subject 'a certain/one', پورش is here the subject (in the manuscript sense either a proper name or 'her son' in older usage), آمد is the simple past 'came/appeared'. چو تابنده ماه is a simile headed by چو 'like/as' modifying that subject ('like the shining moon').
segments.s140-g3: تو گفتی is the reporting phrase 'you said' (تو = you, گفتی = you said in past). گو is the verb 'say' used here as a quotative/imperative marker introducing what was to be spoken, and پیلتن رستم ا‌ست is the content clause 'Piltan is Rostam' (اِست = is). Word order places the reported speech after the reporting verb.
segments.s140-g4: وگر is a conditional/contrastive 'or/if perhaps'. The clause offers alternatives: سام شیرست 'Sam is lion-like / brave' (شیرست = 'lion-like' or 'man of lionly courage') and وگر نیرم است 'or else Nīram is' (here نیرم is presented as the other possibility and است = is). The repetition of وگر signals alternate suppositions.
segments.s140-g5: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خندان شد is 'became smiling' → 'smiled' (خندان = smiling, شد = became/past auxiliary). و links the actions and چهره شاداب کرد is a causative verb phrase 'made her face radiant' (چهره = face, شاداب = fresh/radiant, کرد = made/did). The sequence describes simultaneous/close actions.
segments.s140-g6: ورا is an indirect-object/locative pronoun 'to him/for him', نام = 'name', and the construction نام ... کرد 'to do/make a name' is an idiom meaning 'to name'. تهمینه is the agent (Takhmina) and سهراب is the given name; کرد is the past of 'to do' used here to mean 'she named (him)'. The clause therefore means 'she named him Sohrab'.
segments.s140-g7: چو یک ماه شد is a temporal phrase 'when he became one month (old)' (چو = when, یک ماه شد = lit. 'became one month'). همچو یک سال بود is a simile 'he was like one year (old)' (همچو = like/as, بود = was). Together it says that at one month of age he appeared as if a year old.
segments.s140-g8: This clause uses an archaic locative/possessive sense: برش ... بود functions as 'in/for him there was' or more idiomatically 'he had' / 'in him was'. چون بر رستم زال بود is a comparative 'he was like Rostam Zāl' (چون = as/like, بر رستم زال = upon/about Rostam Zāl, بود = was). The intended meaning is that he resembled Rostam (Zāl) in appearance or qualities.
segments.s140-g9: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the age clause; 'سه ساله شد' is an adjectival/predicative phrase 'became three‑year‑old' (چو + [age] + شد = 'when he turned X'). The main clause 'زخم چوگان گرفت' has زخم (wound) as the patient and گرفت (took/received) as the verb; چوگان names the instrument/agent (a polo mallet), so literally 'a wound from the polo‑mallet he received.'
segments.s140-g10: به پنجم is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the fifth (year)' (classical compressed expression for 'when he was five'); دل تیر و پیکان گرفت literally 'the heart took arrow and shaft(s)' — دل is the affected part, تیر و پیکان are coordinated instruments, and گرفت again means 'received/was struck by.' The subject (he) is omitted and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s140-g11: چو ده ساله شد is again the temporal clause 'when he turned ten.' زان زمین is a classical locative/partitive phrase 'of/from that land' (modern: در آن زمین), and کس نبود is a negative existential 'there was no one.' Word order is archaic (زان زمین before کس نبود).
segments.s140-g12: که introduces a relative clause modifying کس: 'who/that.' یارست is a fused predicate 'is/was a yār (match, companion),' here meaning 'a match' or 'equal.' با او نبرد آزمود literally 'to try battle with him' — با او marks the opponent, نبرد is 'battle,' and آزمود (past form 'tried/tested') yields the sense 'who could test/fight him' (no one existed who could confront him in battle).
segments.s140-g13: بر مادر آمد is 'he came to (his) mother' (بر used as motion/toward). بپرسید is the past tense 'he asked' (classical narrative past), and زوی here means 'of her/from her' (i.e. he inquired of his mother). The clause order follows older Persian patterns (motion verb then reporting verb).
segments.s140-g14: بدو گفت = 'she said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). گستاخ is used as a vocative/adjective 'Impudent one!' and بامن بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive 'speak to me' or 'tell me' (با من 'with me/in my presence' + بگوی 'speak'). The line introduces direct speech addressed to the child.
segments.s140-g15: که introduces the reported content ('that'/'say that'). من is 'I.' چون here functions causally 'since/because' (چون ز همشیرگان برترم = 'because I am superior to my milk‑siblings'), ز is the classical preposition 'of/from' and همشیرگان means 'milk‑siblings' (those who were nursed together); برترم is an adjectival predicate 'I am superior' with the first‑person suffix -م.
segments.s140-g16: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'always/still'; بآسمان (to the sky/heavens) + اندر آید (literally 'comes into') form the verb phrase 'rises up into the sky'; سرم is 'my head' with the possessive suffix -م. The clause therefore literally says 'my head comes up into the sky,' idiomatically 'my head rises to the heavens' (pride/elevation).
segments.s140-g33: Literal word order: numeral + noun + adjective (یاقوت رخشان = “three rubies, radiant”), then the phrase with به introducing the mount/setting (به سه مهره زر = “on/into three golden settings/beads”). In classical Persian به can mark the notion of being placed in/on; رخشان follows یاقوت as a postpositive adjective (= “shining”).
segments.s140-g34: از ایران = a source phrase “from Iran.” فرستاده بودش is a compound past (pluperfect) with فرستاده (past participle / ‘sent’) + بود (auxiliary) and the enclitic ش referring to the thing sent or to the indirect object; پدر at the end is the agent/subject (typical older/poetic word order puts the agent after the verb). Together: “(it) had been sent (from Iran) by his father.”
segments.s140-g35: بدو = the prepositional/enclitic form ‘to him’ (i.e. ‘unto him’). گفت here is the past-tense verb ‘said’ (poetic word order places the pronoun+verb before the subject): literally “to him said Afrasiab this speech.” این سخن = “this speech/this matter,” the direct object of گفت.
segments.s140-g36: نبایدکه is a fused negative-modal ‘must not’ (نباید + که linking clause). داند is the present/subjunctive form of دانستن ‘to know’ governed by the modal—‘must not know’ or ‘should not be known.’ ز سر تا به بن is an idiomatic prepositional span ‘from head to root’ = ‘throughout / from top to bottom’ (ز = from; سر = top/head; تا به = up to; بن = base/root).
segments.s140-g37: پدر‌گر is the conditional sense ‘if (the) father…’ (گر = ‘if’ attached after the noun in older/epic style). شناسد is the verb ‘recognizes/knows’ (here in the protasis: ‘if the father recognizes’). که introduces the subordinate clause; تو is the subject ‘you’; زین نشان = ‘by this token’ (زین here = ‘this’/‘this (object)’ in older usage and نشان = token/sign).
segments.s140-g38: شدستی is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning ‘you became / you will be made’ (second‑person perfect/preterite sense in context). سرافراز is a postpositive adjective ‘honored/proud,’ and گردنگشان (گردن‑نشان) is a compound meaning ‘neck‑ornament / token worn at the neck.’ Together the predicate says he will be made glorious/receive honor in the form of a neck‑ornament.
segments.s140-g39: چو = ‘when.’ داند = ‘he knows / learns’ (present/historical present). بخواندت is a verbal form with the prefix بـ (verbal aspect) + خواند + the object enclitic ت = ‘calls/summons you’ (خواندن can mean ‘to call’ in this sense); نزدیک خویش = ‘near himself’ (نزدیک = near; خویش = his own). The clause expresses a time/result: ‘When he finds out and calls you near to himself…’
segments.s140-g40: دل مادرت = ‘the heart of your mother’ (مادر + possessive suffix ت = ‘your mother’). گردد is the subjunctive/future-like form of گَردیدن/گشتن ‘become / will be’ used here to state the consequence. از درد ریش = ‘from the pain of (her) beard’ literally, but in context idiomatically ‘from grievous pain/sorrow’; the preposition از marks cause. The result: ‘your mother's heart will be stricken/tormented by grief.’
segments.s140-g25: This clause has an archaic/poetic opening word (ازیرا) followed by a nominative phrase and a predicative comparative. سرت is the possessed noun 'your head' (noun + clitic) placed before the comparative phrase ز آسمان; برترست is the predicate ('is higher/is superior') in one-word copular form typical of classical Persian poetry. The preposition ز here functions in the older/poetic comparative sense ('than' / 'from') instead of the modern از.
segments.s140-g26: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('that' / 'for'), giving the reason or origin of the preceding praise. تخم تو is a genitival phrase 'your seed/origin' (noun + pronoun), زان = 'from that' (pointing back to some famed source), and گوهرست is the predicate 'is a gem' (compound predicate in one word). Order is SOV-influenced: subject (تخم تو) → prepositional origin (زان) → predicate (گوهرست).
segments.s140-g27: جهان‌آفرین is the nominal subject 'the World‑Creator / Creator' and تا introduces a temporal clause meaning 'until'. جهان آفرید is a past-tense clause 'created the world' (verb after object in poetic order). The whole chunk functions as the temporal clause setting the time frame for the next statement.
segments.s140-g28: This is the main clause: سواری ('a rider, horseman') is the subject, چو here means 'like/as' introducing a comparison with رستم, and نیامد پدید is a compound verbal expression in the past tense meaning 'did not come forth/appear' (نیامد = 'did not come', پدید = 'appear'). The word order places the comparative phrase immediately after the subject for emphasis: 'a rider like Rostam did not appear.'
segments.s140-g29: چو here functions temporally as 'when'. سام نریمان is a proper-name phrase ('Sam [son of] Nariman'); به گیتی نبود is a past-tense locative clause meaning 'was not in the world' (به used poetically for a locative). The clause as a whole establishes the time/condition for the following statement.
segments.s140-g30: سرش را is the definite object 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش + object marker را). نیارست is an archaic/polite past-form verb used here intransitively or impersonally; in context it functions like 'did not raise/was not granted' (the exact lexical history is archaic). گردون بسود is an agent/benefactive phrase 'the sky/heavens (گردون) to advantage/for profit' and indicates that the heavens did not favorably set or lift his head. Grammatically, the object phrase precedes the verb as is common in older Persian; the final prepositional phrase gives beneficiary/attitude.
segments.s140-g31: یکی نامه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain letter' (یکی here = 'a certain/some'). از رستم gives the source 'from Rostam'; جنگ جوی is an epithet formed by noun + جوِ/جوی ('war-seeker' → 'warrior') written in poetic separation. The whole phrase names the object (a letter coming from Rostam, described as a warrior).
segments.s140-g32: بیاورد is the past tense 'he/they brought (it)'; و is the coordinating 'and'; بنمود is past 'showed/revealed'. پنهان here functions adverbially/adjectivally as 'hidden/secretly' modifying بنمود. بدوی is an obscure/poetic form in this position (its exact meaning in classical usage is uncertain) and appears as an adverbial or qualifier after پنهان; its precise sense is not evident from the supplied lemma list.
segments.s140-g49: برانگیزم is the first‑person singular present/subjunctive (volitional) of برانگیختن, here meaning “I will rouse/raise.” از + گاه is a prepositional phrase ‘from (his) seat/time’; گاه in epic usage can mean the court or seat (here ’seat/position’), and کاووس را is the definite object marked by را — ‘Kavus (as the object)’. The clause therefore reads literally “I will rouse Kavus from his seat.”
segments.s140-g50: از ایران is a source phrase ‘from Iran’; ببرم is the 1sg present/subjunctive of بردن used volitionally ‘I will take/carry away.’ پی طوس را is the object phrase, with پی here functioning as a preposition/adverbial (’after/in relation to’ or simply a connective ‘with/along’) governing طوس (Tūs) which is made definite by را. Together: “and I will carry Tūs off from Iran.”
segments.s140-g51: به رستم دهم: به marks the recipient (‘to/for Rostam’) and دهم is the 1sg present/subjunctive (volitional) of دادن, ‘I will give.’ The list تخت و گرز و کلاه are the three direct objects being given (throne, mace, crown/helm); Persian often lists coordinated nouns after the verb when the recipient is marked with به.
segments.s140-g52: نشانمش is نشان دادن (to show/present) with the object enclitic -مش ‘him,’ so it reads ‘I will show/present him.’ بر گاه is a locative expression ‘on/upon the court (at the court of)’, کاووس شاه is a noun + title in apposition (‘Kavus the king’). The whole clause: ‘I will present him at King Kavus’s court.’
segments.s140-g53: از ایران به توران gives a path or movement ‘from Iran to Turan.’ شوم is the 1sg present/subjunctive of شدن, used here volitionally ‘I will become/go as.’ جنگ‌جوی is a predicative noun ‘warrior’ describing the speaker’s role: ‘I shall become (or go) a warrior from Iran to Turan.’
segments.s140-g54: This clause strings verbs and nouns in a compact, epic style. ابا is the verb ‘to be unwilling/to refuse’; here it functions as ‘I will not shrink/be reluctant’ (negative sense is often implied by context). شاه روی (‘the king’s face/presence’) is the phrase governed by اندر آرم بروی: اندر + آرم (1sg of آرمیدن ‘to rest/abide’) = ‘I will rest/remain in (his) presence,’ and بروی (here 2nd/1st‑person form of رفتن) indicates movement ‘I will go into/approach.’ Taken together the line expresses: ‘I will not be reluctant to face the king; I will enter/abide in his presence.’ Grammatically note the compactness: verbs appear with poetic forms and objects/locations follow without extra particles.
segments.s140-g55: بگیرم is the 1sg present/subjunctive/volitional of گرفتن ‘I will seize/take.’ The following nouns are given in a compact appositive/listing: سر (head) and تخت افراسیاب (Afrasiab’s throne). In epic diction items taken or overcome are often listed without extra particles, so this clause reads ‘I will seize the head and the throne of Afrasiab’ (i.e. I will overthrow him and take his head/throne).
segments.s140-g56: سر نیزه بگذارم: سر (head) is the object, نیزه (spear) is the instrument/place, and بگذارم is the 1sg present/subjunctive of گذاشتن ‘I will place/put.’ از آفتاب literally ‘before/from the sun’ is a poetical locution meaning ‘in front of the sun’ (i.e. publicly, exposed to the daylight). So: ‘I will put his head on a spear before the sun.’
segments.s140-g17: ز تخم کیم وز کدامین گهر is a series of short interrogative noun phrases. ز (ز) is the preposition 'from' in a poetic register; تخم here means 'seed/origin' (lineage); کیم is an interrogative 'whose?/of whom?' placed after the noun (typical older/poetic word order). وز is here the contraction of و + ز ('and from'), joining the two parallel phrases; کدامین is the interrogative adjective 'which', and گهر means 'gem/jewel' used metaphorically for famous lineage.
segments.s140-g18: چه گویم چو پرسد کسی از پدر is a subordinate question clause. چه introduces 'what' (here 'what shall I...'); گویم is first-person present/subjunctive of گفتن used in the sense 'shall I say / do I say' (classical Persian uses گویم where modern Persian would use خواهم گفت or می‌گویم); چو means 'when'; پرسد is third-person present (used for general/hypothetical time) 'asks'; کسی is 'someone'; از + پدر = 'about/of the father' (از marks the topic of the question). Word order places the temporal clause (چو پرسد...) after the main interrogative.
segments.s140-g19: گر این پرسش از من بماند نهان is a conditional protasis. گر = 'if'; این پرسش = 'this question'; از من = 'from me' (i.e., concealed to me); بماند is present/subjunctive of ماندن 'remain' used in the if-clause ('remains'); نهان = 'hidden' (predicate adjective placed after the verb phrase, typical of older word order).
segments.s140-g20: نمانم ترا زنده اندر جهان is the apodosis with a negative verb form. نمانم is ن + مانم = 'I shall not let (something) remain' or 'I will not remain/allow to remain' (negative prefix attached to the verb); ترا is the accusative clitic 'you' placed after the verb (classical order often puts the object after the verb); زنده = 'alive'; اندر جهان = 'in the world'. Together the clause means 'I will not let you remain alive in the world.'
segments.s140-g21: بدو گفت مادر که بشنو سخن is a speech-introducing clause. بدو = 'to him' (prepositional pronoun); گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' (past narrative) introducing direct speech with که; مادر = 'the mother' (subject in the narrative clause). Inside the reported speech, بشنو is the imperative 'listen' and سخن = 'words' (object of the imperative).
segments.s140-g22: بدین شادمان باش و تندی مکن contains two imperatives linked by و. بدین = به + این 'because of/at this' (classical fused form) introduces the reason/ground; شادمان باش is the imperative 'be joyful' (شادمان = joyful, باش = be!); تندی مکن is the prohibitive imperative 'do not be hasty' (تندی = haste/frenzy, مکن = do not do). Word order places the prepositional/causal phrase before the imperative.
segments.s140-g23: تو پور گو پیلتن رستمی is an address plus an instruction. تو = 'you' (topic/vocative); پور = 'son' used vocatively; گو is the imperative 'call/say' (tell people/declare); پیلتن رستمی is a compound/title: 'of Piltan' (place or family name) + رستمی 'Rostami' (belonging to Rostam or of Rostam's line) — together instructing the addressee to take or be given that name/title. Poetic word order places the vocative and imperative compactly.
segments.s140-g24: ز دستان سامی و از نیرمی is a short genitival/etymological phrase giving parentage or origin. ز = 'from'; دستان is the plural/construct 'hands' used idiomatically to indicate 'by the hands of / from the house of'; سامی here functions as a proper name (Sām or Sāmī) identifying the paternal source; و از نیرمی = 'and from nīrmi/tenderness' — this final phrase can be read literally as 'and from tenderness/softness' (an attribute) or as a matronymic/personal name (in poetry names sometimes look like adjectives). The whole phrase lists two sources of origin/character.

sentences.s140-s29: When Rostam is the father and I the son, there should not be any trader in the world.
sentences.s140-s30: When the face of the sun and the moon was bright, why should a star raise a hat?
sentences.s140-s31: From every side a host gathered about him, an assembly which was both jeweled and sword-bearing.
sentences.s140-s21: Sohrab said this: in the world no one keeps these words hidden.
sentences.s140-s22: Great warriors of ancient times now tell the tale of Rostam.
sentences.s140-s23: No lineage has ever produced one like this; what sort of custom was it to hide him from me?
sentences.s140-s24: Now I will raise up, from the Turks, warlike fighters; I will bring forth an endless army.
sentences.s140-s1: When nine months had passed for the king's daughter, a certain son of hers appeared, like a shining moon.
sentences.s140-s2: You said, ‘Say: Piltan is Rostam’ — or else he is Sam the lion, or else he is Nīram.
sentences.s140-s3: When she smiled and brightened her face, she called him Sohrab, Takhmina giving him that name.
sentences.s140-s4: When he was one month old he seemed like a year old; in him he resembled Rostam (Zāl).
sentences.s140-s5: When he turned three years old he received a wound from a polo‑mallet; at five his heart was struck by arrows and shafts.
sentences.s140-s6: When he became ten years old there was no one in that land who could be his match in battle.
sentences.s140-s7: He went to his mother and asked her; she said to him, 'Impudent one, speak to me.'
sentences.s140-s8: Say: 'For I, being superior to my milk‑siblings, my head always rises to the heavens.'
sentences.s140-s17: His father had sent him three radiant rubies set in three golden beads from Iran.
sentences.s140-s18: Afrasiab said to him, “This matter must not become known through and through.”
sentences.s140-s19: If his father should recognize you by this token, you will be made glorious by the neck‑ornament.
sentences.s140-s20: When he learns of it and summons you to his side, your mother's heart will be torn by grief.
sentences.s140-s13: Azira, your head is loftier than the sky, for your seed is from that renowned jewel.
sentences.s140-s14: Until the Creator made the world, no rider like Rostam had come into being.
sentences.s140-s15: When Sam, son of Nariman, was not in the world, the heavens did not raise his head to advantage.
sentences.s140-s16: A certain letter from Rostam the warrior was brought, and he showed (it) secretly.
sentences.s140-s25: I will rouse Kavus from his seat and carry Tus off from Iran.
sentences.s140-s26: I will give Rostam the throne, the mace, and the crown, and present him at King Kavus's court.
sentences.s140-s27: I will go as a warrior from Iran to Turan; I will not be reluctant to face the king and will enter his presence.
sentences.s140-s28: I will seize Afrasiab’s throne, and I will set his head upon a spear before the sun.
sentences.s140-s9: Of what stock and from which jewel — what shall I say when someone asks about your father?
sentences.s140-s10: If this question remains hidden from me, I will not leave you alive in the world.
sentences.s140-s11: The mother said to him, 'Hear these words; be glad about this and do not be hasty.'
sentences.s140-s12: You — call yourself a son of Piltan, a Rostami; (you are) from Sām's hands and from tender nature.
